data.title: SCHD: Be Thankful To Mr. Market For An Opportunity To Buy More
data.description:
SCHD: Little Respite Due To Broad Selloff

I think it’s fair to say that even the so-called more defensively configured Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) didn’t manage to elude the most significant downside that afflicted all sectors across the market (including healthcare or consumer staples plays), as a broad de-risking took place.

Yet, the relative outperformance of SCHD compared to the S&P 500 (SPX) (SPY) since those highs in February has lent credence to the potential for SCHD to attract buyers seeking a shelter from the maelstrom, as President Trump unleashed market carnage with his swift Liberation Day reciprocal tariffs.

Yet, the respite from last week’s “Blink Wednesday” has also helped the S&P 500 recover most of its losses, while the temporary tariffs exemption on a wide range of electronics goods (including smartphones and PCs) are anticipated to bolster the recovery further, potentially altering the risk/reward profile for the SPX against more defensively configured ETFs like SCHD.

First things first, I’ve already enunciated my conviction about SCHD in my previous update in February. I defended the underlying tracking index’s sound portfolio construction, while benefiting from a relatively low P/E. SCHD also has a relatively low exposure to the tech sector (10%), mitigating the tech-led battering that investors turned away from the premium-valued Magnificent Seven stocks, while also benefiting from a relatively solid dividend yield of more than 4.1%.

Yet, with last week’s selloff in the bond markets and rising credit spreads, I don’t think even high dividend ETFs could shield investors from downside risks if the economy were to fall into a recession, as the odds have clearly risen, impinged by the growing uncertainties imputed to Trump’s policy whiplash and ambiguous messaging, making the situation arguably “untenable” for business leaders to engage in long-term planning, suggesting there could be cascading risk scenarios that may not have been fully contemplated for investors.

SCHD's Reconstitution: Mixed Feelings About More Energy Exposure

Notwithstanding the market’s focus on Trump’s policy reversals and scattershot implementation of his tariffs strategies on allies and adversaries alike, some investors might have been bewildered by the changes in SCHD's annual reconstitution based on the underlying Dow Jones U.S. Dividend 100 Index, given the sharp reallocations to the energy and consumer staples (XLP) plays, while lowering the exposure to financials.

Since the reconstitution in March, energy stocks (XLE) have faced a moment of reckoning, as XLE dropped to levels last seen in early 2023, as investors retreated from energy plays as they reacted negatively to upgraded recessionary dynamics, while reassessing the energy trade flows between the US and China, as they are expected to slow to a trickle if not a halt, given the punitive tit-for-tat tariffs imposed on US-China trade.

Unless some sustained reprieve could be achieved in the next few months, energy investors might have to be prepared for a gloomier 2025, as President Trump sets about his “energy dominance” policy imperatives, but with little assurances on how low the Trump administration wants oil prices to fall to. Hence, even though the energy sector remains solidly profitable, while fulfilling SCHD’s underlying requirements through the index methodology, worsening conditions in the sector could stymie a quick recovery of sorts, even if we don’t experience a recession to follow through.

Notably, the allocation out of financials has helped mitigate the downside risks attributed to the potentially worsening credit risks which accounted for more than 17% of the ETF in March before the reconstitution. Based on the latest holdings metric of about 8.3%, it marks a fundamental shift in exposure, as SCHD’s underlying index moves closer to having heavier exposure to energy and consumer staples plays.

While the reallocation to energy seems untimely given the recent developments, I think the more defensive move toward consumer staples (now 20.4% of total exposure) indicates a clear shift toward a less risky construct within a much more uncertain global economic outlook, as Trump seeks to restructure the world’s economic order, while upending the fundamental pillars of the US economy and its institutional underpinnings.

I don’t think the warning from the "bond vigilantes" should be taken lightly, as the markets fired off a series of salvos to the US government to temper their enthusiasm to disrupt the US economy, or possibly face even more dire consequences, potentially resulting in an unwanted and arguably unnecessary financial crisis.

Yet Consumer Staples Seem An "Ok" Bet For SCHD

With that in mind, a move toward consumer staples is still considered apt, as the sector has generally outperformed SCHD during times of tremendous uncertainties. As I annotated in the above chart, we can observe such distinct risk off moves benefiting XLP over SCHD between 2021 and early 2023 (Silicon Valley Bank crisis), and also the recent spurt over the past few weeks.

Yet, I must also note there isn’t a clear trend of outperformance aside from these spikes, suggesting that unless we fall into a debilitating recession or financial crisis, a bullish reversal in the market, leading to a more aggressive return to risk-on sectors could see SCHD underperform.

That being said, I believe several fundamental underpinnings should still help the ETF perform well amid the worsened geopolitical dynamics. I don't think we should take for granted the short-term respite would be enough to temper the structural uncertainties in the market. Hence, it could help to maintain investor interests in SCHD’s robust 4.1% dividend yield, while having more than 80% of its exposure as assessed to possess at least a narrow economic moat. In addition, SCHD also boasts a positive alpha relative to its index and category peers on a five-year basis, with a demonstrably lower beta metric to support its thesis for more risk-averse investors.

And with a P/E of 14.2x, it’s markedly lower than the current 19x for the S&P 500, and also the 23.6x assigned to the Mag 7 stocks. In other words, SCHD investors should face lower risks of sharp multiples compression (though not immune) that could afflict its higher multiples peers much more extensively.

Buy Or Sell SCHD ETF?

Moreover, there are reasons to consider that last week’s bullish reversal has likely marked the end of its recent steep fall from its 2024 highs:

  • The re-test against the $25 level was underpinned by robust buying conviction, helping to validate last week’s reversal and stymying further selling onslaught.

  • SCHD remains in an uptrend with the 200-week moving average (purple line) justifying dip-buying momentum observed in late 2023 and also last week’s lows. So, the dip-buying sentiments have been remarkably consistent.

  • The steepest selloff since 2022 over the past two weeks has shaken out froth in the market and also SCHD, as seen in the capitulation price action. In my experience, these steep plunges usually precede at least a short-term rally, and supported by the ETF’s already oversold stochastics.

Coupled with the fundamental factors elucidated in my commentary earlier, I have confidence that investors looking for a less risky take into a market recovery should consider SCHD as a noteworthy addition to their portfolio.

While the inopportune downside in the energy sector added to the pessimism recently, I have also observed bottoming signals in XLE, aligning with the well-supported lows gleaned in SCHD’s price action last week.

Rating: Maintain Buy.

data.title: Airbnb: A One-Star Review For This Overvalued Business
data.description:
In the years after the pandemic, demand for travel has been robust, with splurging consumers especially bidding up hotel room rates and premium cabins on planes. But in those years as well, the stock market had roared consistently higher: in the wake of Trump’s tariff scares this year, 2025 might play out very differently.

Airbnb (NASDAQ:ABNB), in my view, is one of the most at-risk stocks. The vacation rental site is down roughly 30% over the past year, but a slowdown in bookings growth could make those losses even stiffer.

I last wrote a sell opinion on Airbnb in December, when the stock was trading closer to $135. Since then, Airbnb’s stock has fallen in sympathy with the rest of the stock market, but not nearly enough to correct the stock’s premium valuation multiples. Meanwhile, even though Airbnb’s bookings trends improved in Q4, its rivals saw even better trends. With this in mind, I’m reiterating my sell rating on Airbnb.

There are several main factors driving my pessimism on Airbnb. The first is that we can already see leading indicators of a sharp slowdown in the travel industry. One of the best leading indicators is the fact that airlines from Delta (DAL) to United (UAL) have announced that they’re cutting capacity, citing weak demand trends. Vacationers often book their flights ahead of booking accommodations, so weak advance bookings for flights likely signals a much weaker bookings trajectory for Airbnb as well.

Travel demand, to some degree, is fueled by the “wealth effect” that the stock market rally of the last few years has given us. When daily volatility in trading is now leading to 5%+ swings in the S&P 500, it makes sense that more consumers are hoarding cash and being very careful on discretionary spending ahead of a potential recession.

The second reason I’m bearish on Airbnb is that, despite this weak backdrop, Airbnb still trades at premium valuation multiples. At current share prices near $115, Airbnb trades at a market cap of $71.17 billion. After we net off the $10.07 billion of cash and $1.99 billion of debt on Airbnb’s latest balance sheet, the company’s resulting enterprise value is $63.09 billion.

Meanwhile, for FY25, the company has only guided to revenue and bookings for Q1, which it noted will see a meaningful deceleration in y/y growth rates (to 4-6% growth in revenue) due to the later timing of Easter. It is, however, calling for 34.5% in full-year adjusted EBITDA - which is actually a 2-point reduction in margins, owing to the company’s aim of investing to scale newer offerings (another red flag for Airbnb).

Wall Street consensus is currently calling for $12.18 billion in revenue in FY25, or 10% y/y growth. Applying Airbnb’s guidance margin against that revenue target gets us $4.26 billion in adjusted EBITDA (5% y/y growth), and positions Airbnb’s valuation at 14.8x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA.

For a company that is only expecting single-digit bottom line growth this year, a mid-teens adjusted EBITDA multiple is a lot to ask for - especially when we consider the fact that Airbnb is only slightly cheaper than Booking.com (BKNG), which is growing bookings at a faster pace and has better margins, and much more expensive than Expedia (EXPE), which is growing in-line.

The next major catalyst for Airbnb will be its Q1 earnings release, expected in early/mid-May. I’d take the opportunity to sell Airbnb before a negative reading has the chance to take the stock down further.

Airbnb’s bookings can’t keep up with its rivals, a trend that is likely to spill over to Q1

One very important question bears asking and examining in greater detail: what exactly makes Airbnb special now? In its earlier days, booking an Airbnb was more of a novel experience as other travel aggregators only offered standard hotel rooms. Now, Expedia and Booking both offer vacation rentals, and even more varied types of accommodations like “aparthotels” that Airbnb has relatively little supply of. Airbnb hosts frequently cross-list across platforms, especially Booking.com which has aggressively grown its stock of apartment listings, particularly in urban cores.

Airbnb’s bookings trends have been, at least up to Q4, respectably strong. The chart below shows its bookings growth rates:

In Q4, Airbnb’s bookings grew 13% y/y to $17.6 billion, accelerating 3 points from 10% y/y growth in Q3. As a reminder, Airbnb recognizes bookings when its travelers confirm and book their trips, but revenue is recognized when the trip is actually taken.

Acceleration, particularly in a shaky macroeconomy, is a positive sign. The problem is that Airbnb’s growth improvements aren’t impressive when we compare against its peers.

Expedia, shown in the chart below, showcased the same 13% y/y gross bookings growth rate in Q4 - except Expedia’s growth basically doubled from just 7% y/y in Q3, implying that Airbnb is seeing much weaker seasonality than one of its chief rivals.

Meanwhile, the largest OTA platform Booking.com also saw gross bookings growth nearly double to 17% y/y, from a 9% pace in Q3.

Is there a special factor to Airbnb anymore? I continue to lament the fact that Airbnb has no loyalty program. Booking.com has the Genius program, which offers discounts on stays and perks like free breakfast or late checkout at many participating properties. Expedia’s OneKey platform is arguably better, with anywhere from 1-12% cash back on hotel stays, depending on the property and the customer’s loyalty tier level. I’m a frequent traveler, and I belong to the highest tier status in both Expedia (achieved after 30 nights stayed) and Booking (achieved after 15 unique bookings) - and these rewards offered are the reason I so seldom book via Airbnb now.

Airbnb is touting its investments into newer offerings, like Experiences. But is this truly unique? Booking.com also offers activities, and sends travelers with a list of bookable activities in their destination cities after making an accommodation booking - often with a voucher credit to be used on these activities for members of the Genius program.

Airbnb’s investments into Experiences and other new features have cost the company its margin profile. In Q4, adjusted EBITDA margins fell by 2 points to 31%. We’re still waiting to see if the company’s boosted spending will result in bookings growth rates that outstrip its peers: so far, it hasn’t.

Looking ahead to Q1, the outlook is dim for Airbnb. The company is already expecting deceleration in both bookings and revenue due to the later timing for Easter (around which many Spring Break timings, particularly in Europe, are based), which is falling in late April this year instead of late March in FY24. But beyond this calendar shift, I expect Q1 stock market volatility to take a toll on Airbnb’s bookings and possibly cause downward revisions to guidance and estimates, especially with read-through indicators from airlines cutting their capacity.

Key takeaways

In my view, the recent stock downturn for Airbnb will worsen after the Q1 earnings are released in May. Key things to watch out for in the earnings release are:

  • Will Airbnb's bookings growth rates continue to diversify from its peers?
  • Will Airbnb revise expectations on bookings and revenue, given sharply weaker macro conditions?
  • Can the company showcase that its investments into product development, which have already begun to take a toll on adjusted EBITDA margins and are expected to continue doing so in FY25, yield real improvements in growth?

When we consider the fact that Airbnb's stock is trading at roughly double the valuation of Expedia despite growing bookings at exactly the same pace, I'd prefer to play it safe and sell Airbnb ahead of earnings.

data.title: SCHD: We're Expecting 20%+ Annualized Returns For The Next Few Years
data.description:
It has been a few months since we last covered SCHD, the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (NYSEARCA:SCHD).

Often understood as the 'gold standard' of dividend investing, the fund has long won investor interest and capital based on the ETF's track record of returns, success, and consistent income generation.

In recent years, SCHD has lagged the market somewhat, to the chagrin of some, but this largely reflects - in our view - short-run AI exuberance and a lack of participation in that trend. That is, SCHD's underperformance doesn't have to do with any structural issues, it's simply a result of underinvestment in a few, key stocks that don't pay significant dividends like Nvidia (NVDA)

Over the long run, we're still anticipating robust returns on the back of SCHD's rock solid portfolio construction methodology, distributions, and more.

Recently, the market has sold off aggressively due to investor panic about tariffs, spurred on by the Trump administration. Even more recently (Wednesday, the 9th), stocks bounced back somewhat as Trump announced a pause on the tariffs for most trading partners.

While the recent panic has been rectified - to some degree - by the pause, SCHD is still trading down more than 15% from all-time highs. To us, this represents a massive opportunity, given that dips of this degree, historically, have been a great time to buy into the fund.

Today, we'll explore SCHD's recent performance, give our outlook for investors, and explain why we think the popular ETF could produce returns of 20% or more each year for the next few years.

Sound good? Let's dive in.

SCHD's Construction

Before talking about why we think SCHD is such an attractive looking dip-buy in today's market, it's worth saying a little bit about the fund and how it works.

In short, SCHD is a dividend-focused ETF, built around the Dow Jones U.S. Dividend 100 Index. This means that the fund, essentially, just buys into the stocks from this index, and then rebalances and adjusts every so often to ensure full replication of the index's performance.

As far as indices go, the Dow Jones U.S. Dividend 100 is a good one to copy. On a construction basis, the index selects its' 100 stock positions through a combination of dividend consistency and fundamental quality:

The [index] first screens for 10 years of dividend consistency, along with some minimum liquidity requirements:

Then, the top 50% of stocks from this universe (as ranked by Forward Yield) are given a score based on how they do in the following categories: FCF / Debt, ROE, Forward Yield, and the 5-Year Dividend Growth Rate.

The scores are then sorted, with the top 100 scores coming in as the top 100 companies for the index, subject to a few sector diversification requirements.

All in all, we believe that this selection process lends itself well to selecting high quality companies that can comfortably operate while increasing payouts to shareholders over time.

Buy The Dip

Looking back, this approach has produced - in real terms - some truly excellent results.

On average, SCHD has produced about 13% in annualized returns since inception in 2011, which is great.

Notably, the fund's total returns, when viewed logarithmically, appear incredibly stable and consistent as well:

Sure, the fund has missed out on some appreciation over the last few years, but net of everything, SCHD's stock selection process earns top marks for both returns and risk.

In fact, since inception, SCHD's largest drawdown was during Covid, where it declined roughly 30%, which is less than the S&P 500 and Nasdaq 100 saw on the whole.

Combine this with only a 16% drawdown in 2022, and you're looking at a resilient option for basically all market types:

Speaking of this recent dip, let's break down the recent action.

In short, as of Friday's close, SCHD is trading down roughly 13% from all-time highs, as you can see on the chart above.

Visually, if you scan back over SCHD's performance history, you can see that this is a notable number, as the fund has only really dropped this much a handful of times over the last 14 years - once in 2018, once during Covid, and two other times in 2022 and 2023 during the inflationary period & panic we saw.

Now, if you buy into SCHD when the fund isn't in drawdown on any given weekly close, you'll end up with a following one-year return of about 11.5% and a two-year return of about 25.7%. Not bad.

However, the deeper and deeper into a drawdown you buy, the better and better your returns get, although the frequency of this happening gets lower and lower:

As you can see, as SCHD draws down, the forward returns from buying the fund get better and better. This may sound intuitive, but it's actually not - when some stocks dip, the forward performance is poor or choppy. This is from earnings trendiness and other factors.

SCHD's resilient, mean-reverting behavior is largely a function of the stock selection process picking the strongest companies on the market that are most well equipped to bounce back from jitters. Pack 100 of them into a portfolio, and SCHD's performance makes sense.

In our view, the message from this data is clear - when SCHD dips, buy it.

Now, these buckets are averages, and they aren't prescriptive. Sure, they look at data, but some of these return figures are skewed by the fact that the higher numbers really bring up the average returns over one- and two-year periods.

What do the hard numbers say?

Below, you can see every single weekly close that SCHD has had over the last 14 years where the fund is in a drawdown of 10% or more.

These aren't the numbers 'at the bottom', they're what we have right now - a weekly close where SCHD is trading down by a certain amount from all-time highs:

There are two things of note here.

First, when you buy a dip like this in SCHD, history says that you would have made money 100% of the time over the following year, and 36 out of 38 times over a rolling two-year period.

Plus, the two times that SCHD actually lost money over two years after buying the dip were timed to reflect the Covid bottom - from March of 2018 to March of 2020. Of course, this reflects especially poor timing more than anything else. Not only that, but in that period, SCHD was down less than 10% - a highly positive risk/reward for investors.

Second, looking forward, even if you adjust down for the extreme nature of Covid, this performance record suggests strong gains of 20% or more through the next few years.

Of course, past performance doesn't always lead to future results, but given the portfolio composition and stock selection process, we'd argue that the underlying drivers of performance have been both consistent and repeatable, which gives us confidence in making this call.

Finally, if you're looking at the dividend yield as a means of 'timing' an entry into the fund, then you'll find that now is the best time we've had in the last 5 years:

Taken together, we think an entry into SCHD right now appears highly attractive, and could lead to solid gains over the short, medium, and long term.

Risks

As with anything, SCHD faces some risks that you should be aware of.

First off, the fund's emphasis on dividend payers might mean less growth compared to the broader market, especially when growth stocks are hot. Not only that, but the fund's holdings can change based on index rules, which means that you could end up with a suboptimal portfolio for a given market period, which is what happened over the last two years.

Additionally, SCHD does invest into the broader market, which means that swings will likely impact the fund. If the market crashes, SCHD will likely follow - albeit to a lesser extent. Similarly, while we're advocating for a 'buy the dip' approach to SCHD, the fund may experience further downside from here which nullifies our expectation.

Summary

Overall, though, we think SCHD appears attractive in the current market.

While there are some risks associated with SCHD, the data makes a strong case for buying the recent dip. We're anticipating gains of 20%+ or more in the year following this rating, as a result of the fund's past performance trends, in addition to the portfolio's underlying selection process.

Plus, with a strong yield to boot, the time appears ripe for a 'Strong Buy' in SCHD.

Cheers!

data.title: AMD: Brutal Sell-Off Likely Will Be Reversed
data.description:
Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMD) has been one of the most poorly performing AI chip stocks in the last year, massively under-performing Nvidia (NVDA) which also sold off in a big way in April. The most recent down-leg has been driven by fears related to the tariff chaos that was ignited about two weeks ago and has led to concerns about earnings projections for companies that could be subjected to tariffs, including AMD.

I believe AMD is set for a massive up-surge in 2025 and could more than reverse its most recent valuation losses, especially with the chip maker getting ready to launch new AI accelerators in the second half of FY 2025. With AMD now generating ~50% of its revenues from Data Centers, I expect AMD to potentially grow even faster than Nvidia if product up-take for Instinct MI350 AI accelerator is strong.

Previous rating

In my last analysis of AMD -- The Market Is Wrong -- I said that investors should consider buying the drop in the chip maker’s valuation as its Data Center segment was to ramp up in FY 2025, mainly because AMD was on track to launch new AI accelerators, such as the MI350X. Since then, however, AMD's shares have revalued approximately 14% lower, indicating that my previous bullish call was at least pre-mature.

AMD has seen a surge in its Data Center revenues and increased its free cash flow by 350% in Q4’24, on a year-over-year basis, which indicates to me that AMD's momentum is real and even sustainable. In my opinion, chip stocks especially are highly attractively priced right now and with AMD set to ramp up its Data Center revenue this year, AMD could be a out-performing chip play for investors.

Tariffs are a distraction, AMD's Data Center business is set to crush it in FY 2025

The market crash in the last two weeks has put investors on edge and led to a scary sell-off in tech stocks as well. With sweeping new tariffs getting announced on everything from cars to steel and aluminum, investors have been in for a rough ride. Chip makers were hit especially hard as AI chips are one of the hottest commodities in the market right now. Both Nvidia and AMD are selling their GPUs and AI accelerator to China… a specific tariff target of the U.S. president.

While the tariff spectacle has attracted a lot of investor attention, AMD’s prospects as a chip design company are, however, much more linked with its ability to execute on its growth plans than its vulnerability to potential tariffs. AMD has demonstrated significant momentum in the Data Center segment and the chip design company achieved 50% of total revenues in this business in Q4 '24 (showing 13 PP growth year-over-year). Nvidia's revenues reached $39.3B in FQ4'25, 90% of which came from the Data Center business.

AMD is launching new AI accelerators in FY 2025, however, which could set the chip maker up for strong Data Center-related growth. AMD’s product pipeline calls for the introduction of the Instinct MI350 AI accelerator in mid-2025 while the MI400 AI accelerator is expected to hit shelves in 2026. With new products set to get released in the short to medium term, AMD has a potent catalyst in its portfolio to accelerate its top line growth, and potentially catch up, or even exceed, Nvidia's Data Center-related growth.

Nvidia vs. AMD

(Source: Author.)

Upcoming earnings report for Q1’25

AMD is expected to have generated $0.94 per-share in adjusted EPS in the first-quarter which would show 51% year-over-year growth. In the last 90 days, analysts revised EPS estimates upwards 9 times and downwards 17 times. However, the tariff confrontation has added to recent down-grades, pushing AMD's shares into a new down-leg. I believe we could see a slight earnings beat for Q1 '25 given that AMD's Data Center business has solid growth momentum.

AMD’s valuation, AI spending boom is set to continue in 2025...

AMD’s shares are an absolute steal at its current price of $93 per-share. The chip maker is expected to deliver 41% earnings growth this year while Nvidia is projected to grow its earnings 52%.

While the growth differential is not that big, AMD is significantly cheaper than Nvidia: AMD is trading at a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 15.0X which makes AMD about half as expensive as it used to be, on a forward earnings basis, in the last three years. AMD is not the only chip company that has been brutalized, however, with Nvidia’s shares also trading down significantly in the last two weeks. Currently, Nvidia is trading at a P/E ratio of 19.4X which compares against a price-to-earnings ratio (also on a FY 2026 basis) of 17.3X for Intel, a company that has been bogged down by restructuring challenges and lack of success with its own AI accelerators, called Gaudi 3.

For Nvidia, things are looking very good, however: Nvidia is expected to grow faster than AMD, in terms of long term EPS, and the company just recently started shipping its Blackwell GPU, creating a top line catalyst. The market slide last week and in the week before that is why I am adding to the world's most significant AI GPU player as well: Why I Am Buying The Meltdown.

In my last work on AMD I calculated a fair value in the neighborhood of $212, based off of AMD's historical valuation. I maintain my strong buy rating for AMD in light of the recent market down-turn because I believe that AMD's AI accelerators, as well as Nvidia's GPUs, are absolutely essentially items necessary for the AI and Data Center revolution. As AI applications need GPUs and AI accelerators to run multiple applications simultaneously, and AMD is just about to launch new AI accelerators, the chip design company should be able to experience potent top line, earnings and free cash flow growth catalysts in its Data Center segment in FY 2025.

There is also no evidence that AI investments are slowing down. Alphabet said that it will invest $75B this year in order to boost its AI/Cloud business. According to Gartner, generative AI spending is set to grow massively, by 76.4% Y/Y in FY 2025. AI spending growth is expected to be driven by Services mainly, although Software and Devices are projected to nearly double spending as well this year. Key beneficiaries of this spending boom: Nvidia and AMD.

Risks with AMD

One risk for AMD relates to the company potentially getting tariffed. Since AMD’s chips are designed by Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM), chip exports to China may be classified as Taiwanese, meaning AMD could potentially avoid any tariffs. A much more severe risk for AMD than tariffs, in my opinion, could be weak product up-take with regard to its upcoming Instinct MI350 AI accelerator which is key for the chip maker to accelerate the penetration of the hotly contested GPU/accelerator market.

Final thoughts

The current sell-off in the AI chip market will not last and will likely be reversed at some point in the future, creating a buying opportunity for long term investors. AMD is a top buy for me as the market panics and it is not only the progress that the chip maker has been making in terms of growing its Data Center business that makes shares a buy, in my opinion. AMD could be set for a massive Data Center revenue acceleration in FY 2025, especially as Gartner's AI spending predictions strongly indicate that the market will continue to favor chip design companies with a core focus on GPUs and AI accelerators. Earnings estimates for Q1'25 also indicate that AMD is set for strong, 50%+ Y/Y, earnings growth. AMD is top value in the Data Center/chip business as its shares are currently retailing for about half the firm's 3-year average price-to-earnings ratio while at the same time prospects for AI-driven growth have not deteriorated at all.

data.title: Northwest Natural: Rock Solid Dividend At A Bargain Price
data.description:
It literally pays to have a stable of steady dividend stocks that are more or less immune to what’s going on with the rest of the world. While they’re not as exciting high-flying growth stocks, there’s nothing boring about having a steady dividend check that comes in every three months. John D. Rockefeller famously said that the only thing that gives him pleasure is seeing his dividends come in. While I would hope that other things give you pleasure as well, there is nothing wrong with having passive income that rolls in!

This brings me to Northwest Natural Holding (NYSE:NWN), which I last covered in January, highlighting the value behind this dividend stock with 25 consecutive years of raises and stable and growing earnings. NWN has given investors a 5% total return since my last piece, outperforming the 11% decline in the S&P 500 (SPY) over the same time frame. In this article, I revisit NWN including recent business results and outlook and discuss why it remains a great ‘Buy’ for income, value and growth, so let’s dive in!

Why NWN?

Northwest Natural Holding is a utility company that’s based in Portland, Oregon with a 165-year operating history. It provides natural gas, water and wastewater services across the Pacific Northwest and Texas, and serves nearly 1 million customers in 7 states. As shown below, most of NWN’s revenues are highly regulated, with 98% coming from natural gas and water utilities and mostly residential customer mix.

NWN has seen a strong upward revenue trajectory over the past 10 years, with it rising from $720 million to $1.15 billion over this time frame. Moreover, NWN has been able to maintain a steady EBITDA margin that’s been rangebound between 28% and 33%. As shown below, EBITDA margin has steadily climbed since 2022 from 28% to 30%, all while revenue has climbed over the same time frame (driven in part by the fast-growing SiEnergy gas utility acquisition in Texas).

NWN did report a 10% decline in adjusted EPS last year to $2.33. This was due primarily to regulatory lag for the first 10 months of the year until new rates became effective on November 1st. Moreover, there were near-term dilutive equity issuances to fund growth capital expenditures.

Despite these two factors, which are correctable in the long run, NWN produced solid underlying fundamentals with a utility margin rising by $26 million as a result of the new rates in Oregon. While the rate case took time to approve, it’s the largest-ever in the state of Oregon. This adds $93 million to the revenue requirement and significantly increased NWN’s regulated asset base to $2.1 billion. This ensures continued growth and a recovery on critical capital investments that NWN made.

At the same time, NWN had effective cost management with operations and maintenance expenses declining by $2.1 million last year. Plus, NWN saw robust customer growth in its newer segments. This included 4.6% YoY growth in waste and wastewater customers due to organic expansion and acquisitions. In Texas, NWN’s recent SiEnergy acquisition saw 22% compounded annual growth in customers between 2021 and 2024, signaling the growth potential for NWN in that market.

As a result of the aforementioned catalysts, management is guiding for a rebound in adjusted EPS this year to $2.85 at the midpoint of the range. This represents 22% growth from last year and 10% growth from the 2023 level. This equates to around 5% growth annually and fits with management’s long-term EPS growth target range of 4-6% per year.

NWN also expects combined utility rate base growth of 6-8% annually. This is an increase from previous targets due to expanding business in Texas and water utilities. Rate base growth includes NWN’s Oregon Natural Gas Utility, which has seen a 9% CAGR since 2021. As shown below, NWN opened another rate case this year with a target of 10.4% return on equity and $204 million increase in average rate base. This is supported by a ‘common sense’ voter base, in which 72% oppose banning natural gas in new homes and buildings (up from 63% in 2019).

Importantly, NWN carries a strong balance sheet with $468 million in total liquidity and an A- credit rating with stable outlook from S&P. It has a long-term FFO-to-debt target of ~14%, which sits close to the 15% standard for most utility companies.

Importantly for income investors, NWN currently yields a respectable 4.8%. The dividend is well-covered by an 69% payout ratio based on the midpoint of management’s EPS guidance for this year of $2.85. As noted earlier, NWN has maintained 25 years of consecutive dividend raises. While dividend growth has been light in recent years, it could return to meaningful growth in the 5% range as acquisitions are now in the rearview mirror.

Lastly, I continue to see value in NWN at the current price of $41.26 with a forward PE ratio of 14.2. This sits well below its normal PE of 19.8, as shown below.

At the current valuation, I believe NWN can deliver at least market-level total returns. This is supported by a near-5% dividend yield and my expectations for at least mid-single digit EPS growth annually, driven by the aforementioned rate base growth and the fast-growing Texas market. Plus, any reversion to the mean valuation could be an added total return kicker for investors.

Risks to NWN include its dependency on favorable outcomes in rate cases, which are not entirely in its control. Plus, tariffs could raise the cost of imported raw materials used in its capital projects, thereby impacting profitability. Capacity constraints could impede on NWN’s ability to serve high-demand customers such as data centers, and unfavorable energy policies that promote electrification over natural gas in NWN’s service territories could be a headwind.

Investor Takeaway

Northwest Natural Holding remains an attractive stock for income investors seeking stability, value, and steady growth. With a 4.8% dividend yield, 25 consecutive years of dividend hikes, and a strong regulatory backdrop supporting 6–8% annual rate base growth, NWN is well-positioned to deliver reliable returns. Plus, NWN’s recent Texas acquisition is fueling customer expansion, and EPS is projected to rebound 22% this year, backed by new rates and improved cost controls. Lastly, with a valuation well below historical range, NWN offers both income and potential upside, making it a solid ‘Buy’ in today’s volatile market.
